title: Quick Guide For Dentists and Surgeons
article: An emergency dentist can handle your basic dental needs, but if you've got a more serious issue like needing a tooth pulled, an oral surgeon is your best bet. Wondering how to tell the difference? Keep reading to find out!

title: Quick Guide For Dentists and Surgeons
article:
1. Do You Know About Dental Emergency?

If you have a dental emergency, call your dentist right away. But what exactly counts as a dental emergency? Things like sudden tooth pain, serious mouth injuries, a swollen jaw, or worsening infections all need prompt attention. Your dentist will check your mouth or refer you to a great oral surgeon for treatment.

title: Quick Guide For Dentists and Surgeons
article:
2. Do You Need A Dentist Or An Oral Surgeon?

Your regular dentist handles routine dental care like check-ups, professional cleanings, fillings, sealants, and overall gum care. Some of them can also perform minor oral surgeries. But if you need a tooth extraction or need to rebuild bone and gum tissue, you'll want to see an oral surgeon. They can handle everything from wisdom teeth and impacted teeth to jaw misalignment and bone loss. Plus, they treat chronic conditions caused by oral issues.

title: Quick Guide For Dentists and Surgeons
article:
3. Why Might You Need Oral Surgery?

· If your tooth can't heal or is too damaged, causing crowding, your dentist might suggest extraction. An abscess reaching the root requires immediate treatment. Late-stage gum disease, overcrowding, or tooth loss from sports or accidents are also reasons for extraction.

· Many people get their wisdom teeth removed. If you're getting orthodontic treatment, you might need a tooth pulled for extra space. For ongoing jaw pain, your dentist can check if jaw surgery could ease chewing.

· Getting dental implants is a great way to bring back your smile. And an oral surgeon can offer you a bunch of options to choose from.

· Oral surgeons can also help with removing tumors or treating oral cancer. If you're dealing with advanced or recurring cancer, they'll combine tumor removal with your ongoing cancer treatment.

title: Quick Guide For Dentists and Surgeons
article:
4. What Should You Expect For Tooth Extraction Surgery?

If your dentist says you need an oral surgeon, it's because a simple extraction won't work. For just one tooth, they'll use local anesthesia to numb the area. But if you're getting multiple teeth out, you'll probably be put to sleep with general anesthesia. The surgeon will use special tools to remove the tooth from your jawbone and gums. This leaves a little hole, which they'll pack with gauze to stop the bleeding. Stitches will either dissolve on their own or be taken out when you're healed up.

title: Quick Guide For Dentists and Surgeons
article:
5. What Should You Do After A Tooth Extraction?

After getting a tooth pulled, it's super important to take good care of your teeth and gums to avoid dry sockets. Take it easy for the first 24 hours and try to keep activity to a minimum. If you're feeling pain, you can take some painkillers to help with that, and using an ice pack can reduce any swelling. Stick to soft foods for a bit, and make sure to keep your mouth clean and healthy.
